entry.title: Session 1 Recap: Unpacking the Autonomous Enterprise for Architects
entry.content:
On 1 June 2026, the Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise webinar series officially launched with its opening session together with Mark Smith and Paul Kurchina,  where they provided a strategic overview designed to set the foundation for sessions that follow!

This series is built by architects, for architects. Running weekly, from 1st June through to 20 August, it is designed to help Enterprise Architects make sense of the announcements coming out of SAP Sapphire 2026 from the SAP Business AI Platform and Joule Work to the SAP Autonomous Suite, Knowledge Graph, AI agents, and governance — and understand what they mean in practice for enterprise architecture, data foundations, and transformation.

Register here to get access to the slides recordings and join the following sessions: https://events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html

"The Business AI Platform and Why Foundations Come First"

- Mark Smith

Mark Smith set the strategic context for the series, drawing directly from conversations with customers and partners at SAP Sapphire in Orlando and Madrid.

The Problem: Enterprise AI Is Stuck

Mark opened with a clear framing of the current reality. Three-quarters of enterprises running AI pilots have no clear path to scale. Nearly half cannot get past data quality and access issues. The investment is real, but the results are not. The root cause, he argued, is what he calls the context deficiency — three gaps that keep enterprise AI stuck in pilot mode:

  1. Business process context. Generic models can read data, but they cannot reason over how a business actually runs. They see tables, not operations.
  2. Data connection. AI is layered on top of disconnected, outdated systems and asked to be intelligent about a business it can only see in fragments.
  3. Governance. AI that cannot be audited or controlled does not get deployed into the core of the enterprise. It gets demonstrated. There is a difference.

 

SAP's Answer: Three Foundations Already in Place

Mark then laid out SAP's structural differentiation, not as a roadmap, but as deployed capability:

  1. Deep Process and Industry Knowledge. Fifty years of business process expertise encoded in SAP applications, the Knowledge Graph, and purpose-built models. This is not a marketing number — it is the reason SAP's models deliver 3.5 times higher prediction quality than generic LLMs, grounded in 7.3 million data fields covered by the Knowledge Graph.
  2. Semantically Rich Business Data. SAP data carries business meaning by default. A customer is linked to a contract. A material is tied to an order. A supplier is scored for risk. AI reasons over real business context across both SAP and non-SAP systems.
  3. Enterprise-Grade Governance. SAP has been in governance, risk, and compliance longer than most AI companies have existed. Governance is not a feature added on top it is foundational to the architecture.

The SAP Business AI Platform

Mark introduced the SAP Business AI Platform launched at Sapphire as the foundation for how enterprises will build, connect, and run AI that truly understands their businesses. It is organised around three pillars:

  • Build. A single unified environment for building AI agents, applications, and workflows grounded in business context — using low code or pro code, fully integrated with SAP and non-SAP systems. Joule Studio, the SAP Integration Suite, and integrations with tools like Claude and Cursor enable developers to build from where they are today.
  • Contextualize and Reason. Only SAP can ground AI in fifty years of business process expertise plus the millions of hours organisations have invested in their own implementations. The data fabric serves as the trusted knowledge core, connecting data, processes, and semantics so agents reason accurately and avoid hallucinations.
  • Govern. Scale without control creates exposure. Governance is not a layer on top but the command centre for enterprise-wide data and AI agent governance — from planning through production, with full auditability, lifecycle control, and secure execution.

The Autonomous Enterprise Vision

 

Mark closed his segment by framing the Autonomous Enterprise as SAP's North Star: the future where AI transforms how people work and how processes run by grounding decisions in real-time intelligence, automating end-to-end workflows, and proactively improving every function. Five elements work together to make it real:

  1. Joule : the engagement layer and front door, where users express intent and Joule orchestrates agents and systems to make it happen.
  2. The SAP Autonomous Suite : the operational core, running the business across five domains: Finance, Spend, Supply Chain, HCM, and Customer Experience.
  3. Industry AI : deep industry intelligence bringing sector-specific process logic, data context, and regulatory rules into AI-powered solutions tailored to each industry.
  4. The SAP Business AI Platform : the foundation providing context, unified data, reasoning, and governance in one place.
  5. Agent-led transformation : the path from where organisations are today to the autonomous model, without stopping the business to transform the business.

The message to architects: people set the direction, AI executes. This is the shift from recording what happened to helping execute what is next.

 Register here to get access to the slides recordings and join the following sessions: https://events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html

The Context System and the Architect's Starting Point

I took second part of the session deeper into architectural territory, introducing a framework that will underpin much of the series: the Context System.

Why Context Is Everything

We framed the fundamental shift: this is not task automation. Organisations are now delegating decision-making and judgment to systems that operate through language-like capacity. The critical differentiator is whether your solution has the right context, not just data, not just process information, but a complete context system that includes all the deviations, exceptions, and organisational knowledge that define how work actually gets done. SAP's fifty years of running ERP systems for the world's largest organisations has produced exactly this: a right to win built on deep, operational context available across the full stack.

Five Layers of the Context System

We unpacked the Context System as five distinct architectural layers as written by John Santic in his detailed writeup on The Context System.

1. Data Context — What Happened The foundational layer: transactions, records, orders, invoices, inventory states, financial postings. All post-facto — the transaction has already occurred. SAP Business Data Cloud, combined with the Demeo acquisition (Apache Iceberg capabilities) and BDC Connect, forms the starting point for this layer.

2. Semantic and Knowledge Context — What Does It Mean Data tells you what happened. This layer tells you what it means. Customer versus account versus ship-to — an agent does not inherently understand the difference. The Knowledge Graph and SAP's acquisition of WalkMe (referred to as "Rails" in the session) provide entity understanding, master data relationships, and lineage across systems.

3. Process and Predictive Context — How Work Gets Done The exact process followed by people and systems: approvals, handoffs, bottlenecks, deviations from the intended path. SAP Signavio provides the process intelligence, while Prior Labs' tabular foundational models add predictive capabilities. This is also where the concept of Agent Mining emerges understanding the exact behaviour of agents within systems to enable self-learning and improvement.

4. Organisational and Governance Context — What Is Allowed Today, there is no standard way of governing across different agents. The SAP AI Agent Hub, built on top of LeanIX, provides the capability to govern, provision, observe, and manage every agent across the landscape — whether SAP or non-SAP. This brings the operational and governance context needed for full lifecycle management.

5. Intent and Prescriptive Context — The Company Memory The operational meaning of everything an organisation does. How humans act, and how agents need to serve alongside them. This is what Christian Klein described as the "company memory" in his Sapphire keynote — the layer that enables humans and agents to work together with full contextual understanding.

 We also highlighted four assets already available on the SAP Architecture Center for architects ready to start:

  • AI-Native North Star Architecture — how SAP envisions the convergence of AI, data, and applications across the Business AI Platform, Business Data Cloud, agents, autonomous domains, and Joule as the unified engagement layer.
  • The AI Golden Path — a guided path from idea to production-ready applications, including detailed decision trees for when to use agents vs. deterministic applications vs. Knowledge Graph vs. HANA Vector Engine vs. agentic workflows vs. code-based agents.
  • Agentic AI Architecture Pattern — interoperability between Joule and third-party agents, including details on Agent Hub, Joule Interface, and Agent Gateway.
  • Data Architecture with Business Data Cloud — how to extract data from SAP systems without moving it (zero-copy, delta share protocol), modernise BW landscapes, and build the data layer.

 

Paul Kurchina served as the session's connector, opening the series, framing the philosophy, moderating the audience Q&A, and linking the session to the broader community programme.

The Philosophy: Learning Out Loud Together

Paul emphasised that this series is not a broadcast, it is a shared learning journey. The Autonomous Enterprise was launched just weeks before this session, and everyone including SAP's own teams is learning and growing together. The series is designed to evolve based on participant feedback, with new sessions added as needs surface.

Live Q&A Highlights

Paul moderated four questions from the audience, answered by Mark Smith:

Q: Where is the biggest gap between where customers are today and autonomous operations? Most organisations are experimenting with AI, but when asked who is operating autonomously, 95% of hands go down. The gap is in InfoSec sign-off, understanding of MCP servers interacting with core processes, and the absence of event-driven architecture. Without AI foundations in place, organisations remain stuck in pilots.

Q: Is Clean Core a prerequisite for agent-led transformation? Clean Core makes AI scalable, but the approach must be balanced combining AI adoption with a path to simplification. AI can actually accelerate modernisation and reduce technical debt. The key is identifying which process domains can be automated and building a holistic roadmap from current state to the autonomous enterprise.

Q: What can SAP do that hyperscalers cannot? SAP's true differentiator is grounding AI in how businesses actually run — fifty years of process expertise, the Knowledge Graph, and purpose-built models like RPT 1.5 that are pre-trained with business context. Combined with fully managed governance, lifecycle control, and secure execution via the SAP AI Agent Hub, this provides what external AI providers structurally cannot replicate.

Q: What should every architect do in the next 90 days? Get the foundations in place for agentic execution. Move from pilots to repeatable patterns for enterprise execution. The Business AI Platform provides those foundational elements — and the design-time environment is free of charge, removing barriers to building, testing, and prototyping.

Register here to get access to the slides recordings and join the following sessions: https://events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html

What Comes Next

The in-person tour. In addition to the webinar series, a six-city in-person learning tour is running across North America (Palo Alto, Atlanta, Dallas, Toronto, Newtown Square, and Calgary) bringing architects together face-to-face to discuss what they are learning through the series.

 Your can register for the In-Person Experience here: https://events.sap.com/eaa-aet/en_us/home.html

The series will adapt based on participant input. Surveys follow each session, and new topics or deeper dives will be added based on what architects tell the team they need most. You can still provide feedback here if you have not done already: Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise Webinar Series – Fill out form

What's Up for the Next Session:

The next session on 10 June will focus on what SAP's evolved portfolio means for architects together with Mohamed Abdel Hadi, Habeebuddin Mohammed and Jason Luo examining how the full portfolio comes together (Joule, Joule Work, the SAP Autonomous Suite, Industry AI, and the Business AI Platform) and what it means for architecture in an agentic world. We will also cover How SAP Data and AI services help you craft the right architecture!

Thank you to everyone who joined and hope you continue to support us through this webinar series!

entry.title: Preventive Measure Reporting—Greater Transparency Into Cloud Incident Follow-Up
entry.content:
A key piece of SAP's ongoing commitment to improving your cloud experience—not just during a disruption but long after they’re resolved—is ensuring you have full visibility into how incidents are handled via SAP for Me. To further improve the process, we’re excited to announce Preventive Measures Reporting is now available in SAP for Me’s Systems & Provisioning Dashboard. Preventive Measures Reporting gives you direct, real-time access to the preventive measures identified during root cause analyses (RCAs) following cloud incidents.

When a cloud incident occurs, we conduct a thorough root cause analysis to determine what happened and what steps are needed to prevent it from happening again. With Preventive Measure Reporting, you don’t have to wonder whether those corrective actions are being followed through upon. You can track everything directly from SAP for Me.

We built this with four key goals in mind:

Real-Time Visibility lets you monitor the status of preventive measures identified in root cause analyses at any time, directly through SAP for Me.

Enhanced Post-Incident Transparency gives you a clear insight into how SAP addresses and resolves cloud incidents after closure, building greater trust and accountability.

It elevates the overall customer experience during and after a cloud incident through transparent and timely communication.

And streamlined tracking and validation gives you simplified preventive measure completion confirmation.

Preventive Measure Reporting represents a significant step forward in SAP’s commitment to transparency and customer trust. By providing customers with direct access to corrective actions, SAP empowers its customers to stay informed and hold all parties accountable throughout the entire incident lifecycle—including who owns them, what they entail, and when they are expected to be completed.

Each preventive measure in SAP for Me includes details on:

Attribute

What it tells you

Measure

A description of the preventive measure and the specific improvements we’re implementing.

Owner

Who’s responsible for the measure—SAP, a third-party vendor like a hyperscaler, or customers like you?

Status

Whether the measure is currently “Work in Progress” or “Completed.”

Estimated Completion

The quarter we’re projecting the measure will be done. But the projected end date might change depending on requirements the measure owner sets.

You can currently see preventive measure statuses in SAP for Me for:

  • SAP Ariba Procurement
  • SAP Ariba Sourcing
  • SAP Business Network
  • SAP Customer Experience
  • SAP Enterprise Cloud Services
  • and select SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) services

We're introducing new products and services to SAP for Me to bring you greater visibility of incident statuses and their related preventive measures. For now, though, visit SAP for Me or reach out to your SAP account team for questions or to provide feedback.

entry.title: Re: Clarification on Data Source During OPD Preparation S...
entry.content:
Dear @watertec,

In Public Cloud this behavior is expected and is related to the output item status, not to OPD itself. In Preparation is not an OPD stage. OPD / Output Parameter Determination only determines output parameters such as output type, receiver, channel, form template and related settings. It does not freeze the business content printed on the form

More details below:

SAP Help – Output Parameter Determination

For output items with status In Preparation or To Be Output, SAP confirms that the PDF preview is rendered using current/transient document data. Therefore, if the form field is based on the current material/product description or another master-data-derived value, a later change in master data can still be reflected on the form before the output is completed

More details below:

SAP KBA 3632762 – Custom fields are blank when the output status is 'Completed' on sales documents

SAP KBA 3224780 – Output preview of sales document is blank when in status 'Completed'

SAP KBA 3440654 – Output preview for output item with status “Completed” is blank

There is no universal list of all master data fetched in this status because it depends on the business object, form template and form data provider. In practice, this can include values such as product/material description, language-dependent product text, business partner/address data or document/item texts, depending on the form design

More details below:

SAP KBA 2825919 – How to get the form data XML for a business document

SAP KBA 3426182 – How to get the XML structure for a form template

The recommended approach is to not treat In Preparation as a frozen print state and to complete/send the output only when the document is final. If the printed material description must be fixed, the form should print a stable document-level value, for example the item description stored on the document or a custom print description field populated at document creation/release, instead of a live master-data-derived description

More details below:

SAP Learning – Using Key-User In-App Extensibility to Customize Output Management Templates

Summary:

OPD determines output parameters, but it does not freeze form content. While the output item is In Preparation, the form can still use current/transient data, so master-data changes such as material description changes may appear on the printout. To avoid this, complete outputs promptly or adapt the form to print a frozen document-level description

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: What is your opinion on Document AI - line item capab...
entry.content: Sometimes, a document may show duplicate line items during the initial extraction. This happens because multiple extraction models run simultaneously in the background, which can occasionally cause duplicates when the results are merged. However, once you provide feedback and correct the data, the system learns from it, and future line item extractions will be significantly more accurate.

entry.title: Re: Released API DDLS Was Deleted ATC Error in SAP S/4HAN...
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question.

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether it helps you resolve your issue:

If you are facing compatibility ATC errors due to deleted CDS views in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, here are some steps and recommendations to address the issue:

Identifying the Snapshot

  1. Manage API Snapshots  App: Use the ‘Manage API Snapshots’ app to view historical snapshots. Although the CDS views are not found in the current SAP Default snapshot, they might exist in older snapshots. For more information, see .SAP Help Portal: Creating API Snapshots

  2. Snapshot Details: Check the details of each snapshot to identify which one contains the deleted CDS views. You can view the change history and extracted APIs within each snapshot.

Removing Compatibility Findings

  1. Set Snapshot to ‘Not Check Relevant’: If the CDS views were never delivered to customers and are causing compatibility issues, you can set the default snapshot to ‘Not Check Relevant’. This will prevent the compatibility checks from being triggered by these deleted APIs.

  2. Delete or Regenerate Snapshots: If possible, delete the snapshot containing the deleted APIs or regenerate the snapshot to exclude these APIs.

The following SAP Community blog post may also add some helpful insight:
How to mitigate missing publicly released SAP APIs – The ABAP Cloud API enablement guide

I hope this information helps.

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: S/4HANA Cloud vs On-Premise for a Large Enterprise: Which Option Makes More Sense?
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

We are currently evaluating SAP S/4HANA Cloud and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise for a future SAP implementation in a large enterprise.

Our company already has an established infrastructure, including servers, storage systems, and backup solutions, so no major additional hardware investment would be required for an on-premise deployment. However, most consulting partners we have spoken with strongly recommend the Cloud option.

Given this situation, I would appreciate feedback from people with real project experience.

For a large-scale enterprise, which option would you consider more suitable regarding:

Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)
Customization and development flexibility
Integration with existing systems
Security and data governance
Long-term maintenance and operational effort
Performance and scalability
If the infrastructure is already available, what are the key advantages of S/4HANA Cloud compared to S/4HANA On-Premise?

I would be especially interested in hearing from organizations that have evaluated both options or have experience with large-scale implementations.

Thank you in advance for your insights.

entry.title: Building a Sales Order Backlog Report Using Custom CDS Views (Sales + Billing)
entry.content:
Amartya Ghosh Dastidar, Senior SAP Developer


Introduction

Core Data Services (CDS) views are central to data modelling in SAP S/4HANA. A frequent requirement in the Sales and Distribution (SD) area is a sales order backlog report — the value and quantity of customer orders that have been placed but not yet invoiced. This post explains how to build such a report using custom CDS views that combine the Sales and Billing domains, covering the key techniques involved: joins, aggregation, calculated fields, type casting, null handling, and the correct treatment of cancelled and pro-forma invoices.

Prerequisites

  • SAP S/4HANA system with ABAP Development Tools (ADT) in Eclipse
  • Basic knowledge of ABAP CDS and SQL
  • Familiarity with SD documents (sales orders, billing documents) and the display transactions VA03 / VF03

Understanding the Sales Order Backlog

In SD, a customer order flows through a chain of documents before revenue is recognised:

Sales Order  --->  Outbound Delivery  --->  Billing Document

            (delivery backlog)        (billing backlog)

The term “backlog” can refer to either of two gaps in this chain:

  1. Delivery backlog — orders placed but not yet delivered.
  2. Billing backlog — orders placed but not yet invoiced.

The report described here implements the billing view, defined as Backlog = Ordered Value − Billed Value. This definition determines the data model, because it requires the Sales domain and the Billing domain to be combined rather than reading open order quantities alone.

Standard CDS Views Used

The report is built on top of two released standard CDS views:

  1. I_SalesOrderItem — sales order line items (ordered quantity and net value).
  2. I_BillingDocumentItem — billing document line items (invoiced quantity and net value), which reference the originating sales order line via SalesDocument and SalesDocumentItem.

Solution Architecture

The solution uses a two-view (layered) design:

  1. ZIC_LIVEBILLING (helper view) — returns only valid, non-cancelled invoices per sales order item.
  2. ZIC_SALES_BACKLOG (main view) — joins the sales order item to the helper, aggregates billing, and calculates the backlog.

The layering is necessary because the cancellation logic requires a self-join that cannot be expressed inline within an aggregating view on all CDS compiler releases. Isolating it in a helper view also keeps the cleaning logic in one place and results in a cleaner, more maintainable model.

Step 1: Create the Helper View (ZIC_LIVEBILLING)

This view filters the billing document items down to genuine, live invoices — excluding pro-forma documents, cancellation documents, and cancelled originals.

Explanation

  1. SDDocumentCategory = 'M' — keeps only invoice-type documents; excludes pro-forma invoices.
  2. CancelledBillingDocument = '' — excludes the cancellation documents themselves.
  3. The self-join on C.CancelledBillingDocument = B.BillingDocument links each invoice to any document that cancelled it. The condition C.BillingDocument is null then keeps only invoices that were never cancelled, removing the cancelled originals.

Step 2: Create the Main View (ZIC_SALES_BACKLOG)

The main view joins the sales order item to the cleaned billing data, aggregates the billed values, and derives the backlog.

Key Techniques Explained

  1. Aggregation with GROUP BY — a single order line can be billed across several invoices (partial billing). Summing the billing per order key collapses these into one row and prevents the ordered value from being multiplied.
  2. Type casting — currency amounts of different internal lengths cannot be subtracted directly. Casting all amounts to abap.curr(23,2) (and quantities to abap.quan(13,3)) ensures type compatibility.
  3. COALESCE for null handling — an order line with no invoice returns null from the join; coalesce(..., 0) converts null to zero so the arithmetic remains valid.
  4. Calculated fields — the backlog quantity and value are derived inline as ordered minus billed.
  5. Session date — dats_days_between(RequestedDeliveryDate, $session.system_date) produces the number of days a line is overdue, enabling aging analysis.

Business Logic: Counting Only Valid Billing

Accurate backlog depends on summing only genuine, live invoices. Three categories must be excluded:

  1. Pro-forma invoices — not real sales; excluded via SDDocumentCategory = 'M'.
  2. Cancelled invoices — a cancelled invoice and its cancellation document both remain in the table; the self-join in the helper view removes both, leaving only the live invoice.
  3. Rejected order items — lines with a rejection reason are excluded via SalesDocumentRjcnReason = ''.

Note: the field that stores the cancellation reference can vary by release and may sit on the cancellation document rather than the original. The self-join approach is robust to this, because it identifies cancelled documents from the relationship rather than from a single status field.

Validation

The view should be validated against the source documents (VA03, VF03, and document flow) across the main data patterns:

  • Never billed — no invoice in the flow; backlog equals the full order value.
  • Partially billed — invoice covers part of the order; backlog equals the remaining value and quantity.
  • Fully billed — invoice equals the order; backlog is zero.
  • Cancelled invoice — only the live invoice is counted; backlog reflects the remaining value.

Where source data itself is inconsistent (for example a negatively priced item or a mis-keyed invoice amount), the view reflects the source faithfully. A reporting view should not silently correct source data; such cases are data-quality issues to be flagged separately.

Standard vs Custom CDS Views

Standard delivery/open-order backlog content is appropriate where a vanilla delivery backlog with standard fields is required. A custom view is justified when the requirement differs, as in this case:

  • The metric is ordered − billed (a billing backlog), which standard content does not frame the same way.
  • It combines Sales and Billing into one pre-shaped row with the specific fields and aging logic required for reporting.
  • It encodes system-specific billing rules (pro-forma and cancellation handling) validated against actual documents.

The trade-off is ongoing maintenance. Where standard content satisfies the same definition, it should be preferred to reduce maintenance overhead.

Key Points to Remember

  • Clarify the backlog definition (delivery vs billing) before designing the view.
  • The SQL view name is limited to 16 characters.
  • Aggregate one-to-many relationships before joining to avoid duplication.
  • Cast all amounts and quantities to a common type before arithmetic.
  • Exclude pro-forma, cancelled, and rejected documents from billed totals.
  • Use a layered design when complex filtering cannot be expressed inline.
  • Validate the output against source documents before release.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 07 - Authorization annotations
Re: warning message show via API create outbound delivery
entry.content:
Yes why you see this difference is because, in the API scenario, the framework is different. The API only returns error messages that block processing. Warning messages are not returned in the API response payload — they are consumed internally, and the delivery is created successfully. This is standard behavior in SAP Public Cloud APIs and warnings are not exposed to external consumers, only errors are.

Options to achieve your requirement

1) Convert Warning to Error (if business-critical)
In your BAdI implementation, raise the message as an error instead of a warning. This will stop the delivery creation and return the error message in the API response.

2) Custom Messaging via Extension Fields
Enhance the API response by populating a custom field (e.g., “ValidationMessage”) in the delivery header/item. Your BAdI can set this field when stock is insufficient. The API consumer (Postman or integration system) can then read this field as a “warning.”

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 06 - Project specific configuration
Re: Baseline date and net due date mismatch in the Import...
entry.content: The “1” comes from the payment term definition (100% due in one installment). It does not cause the mismatch. The shifts you see are due to baseline date calculation rules and factory calendar adjustments, even on non-holiday dates.

entry.title: Max Success Forums EMEA 2026 – Event Recap
entry.content:
From Dublin to Cape Town, our exclusive series of Max Success Forums in EMEA brought customers closer to SAP’s latest innovations and direct expertise from SAP executives and specialists. The events dive deep into key subjects such as the Max Success Plan, the newly announced Autonomous Enterprise, including Joule, SAP Autonomous Suite, Industry AI and SAP Business AI Platform, as well as SAP Business Data Cloud – building on the major themes of the Max Summit. We are pleased to share highlights and impressions below:

Netherlands, ‘s-Hertogenbosch, April 23 

  • The Forum attracted 45 customer attendees from 28 different companies that engaged in sessions on SAP’s stack evolution, the new service offerings with success plans and end-to-end business transformation. A highlight was the Customer Exchange session with the CIO of Lipton and an interactive Design Thinking Workshop on AI. 
  • Relive the event with our recap video.  

Ireland, Dublin, April 28 

  • In Dublin, the Forum made a powerful impact as it delivered rich insights into SAP’s innovations and service offerings for 22 customer attendees from 10 companies. We also had the pleasure to host two amazing customer conversations with Aptiv and Glanbia on their partnership with SAP Services and Support and why they selected the success plans. 
  • Relive the event with our recap video.  

Italy, Milan, May 5 

  • Bringing together 49 customer attendees, the Forum offered a variety of sessions with SAP executives and SAP experts on AI and SAP Business Data Cloud with real customer experiences.

entry.title: EWA Dashboard – Data Footprint Forecast Not Displayed (SAP for Me)
entry.content:
After reviewing the KBA 0003539329 - Data Footprint Forecast is Missing in the EWA Workspace, we understand that the forecast may not be generated if the memory usage has been stable over the past three months. However, even if the past three months have been stable, there is still a possibility that the usage may change in the coming months.

Given that the system only starts showing forecast data after a change occurs and sufficient variability is observed (which could take another few months), this behavior seems a bit delayed from an operational perspective.

Therefore, we would like to ask:

  • Is there any way to generate or simulate a forecast based on the current data, even if the historical usage has been stable?
  • Alternatively, are there any tools, configurations, or best practices that allow us to proactively estimate future data footprint without waiting for variability in the actual data?

We appreciate your support and look forward to your advice.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: EWA Dashboard – Data Footprint Forecast Not Displayed...
entry.content:
Hi Customer，

Greetings from SAP.

Unfortunately, there is currently no configuration or option to force-generate or simulate a forecast when historical data is stable in EWA Dashboard. This is by design.

As you may understand, the forecast function in SAP HANA DB depends on having adequate historical variability to construct a meaningful trend model. When usage has been stable for an extended period, the system does not generate a forecast due to the lack of a discernible trend to extrapolate.

However, we recommend use the following approach, which should help estimate the future hardware requirements of the HANA database.

SAP Quick Sizer — use SAP's official sizing tool for capacity planning based on business growth projections

Here are some materials for your reference:

  • Sizing guide as available :http://www.sap.com/sizing → Sizing Guidelines
  • SAP HANA Quick Sizer

Thank you.

Sincerely,

SAP Support

entry.title: SAP Meetup Tbilisi
entry.content:
The recent SAP Community Meetup provided a valuable opportunity for SAP professionals, transformation leaders, architects, consultants, and technology enthusiasts to come together and engage in meaningful discussions about the future of enterprise technology.

One of the greatest strengths of the SAP community is its commitment to collaboration and knowledge sharing. Events like these provide a platform for professionals to exchange ideas, discuss emerging trends, and learn from real-world transformation experiences. They remind us that successful SAP transformations are not defined solely by technology, but by the people behind them—their expertise, collaboration, continuous learning, and willingness to share insights that drive meaningful business outcomes.

Throughout the meetup, participants explored a wide range of topics, including SAP S/4HANA transformations, cloud adoption strategies, AI-driven innovation, business process optimization, and the evolving role of technology in delivering sustainable business value. What made the discussions particularly valuable was the openness with which participants shared lessons learned, challenges encountered, and practical approaches to delivering successful transformation outcomes.

The event once again demonstrated the strength of the SAP ecosystem and the importance of creating opportunities for professionals to connect, learn, and grow together. The quality of the conversations and the diversity of perspectives highlighted the collaborative spirit that continues to drive innovation across the SAP community.

A special thank you goes to our partners and sponsors — New Vision University, EPAM, PBD Experts, AgentPark LLC, and Synora Consulting — for their support and commitment to fostering professional development, innovation, and knowledge sharing. Their contribution was instrumental in making this event possible and creating an environment where meaningful connections and valuable discussions could take place.

Appreciation is also extended to the organizers, speakers, and volunteers whose dedication and hard work ensured a successful and engaging experience for all participants.

As organizations continue to navigate increasingly complex digital transformation journeys, community-driven initiatives such as this become more important than ever. They provide a platform not only for learning about emerging technologies and industry trends but also for building relationships, exchanging best practices, and strengthening the network of professionals driving transformation across industries.

Thank you to everyone who contributed to making this meetup a success. I look forward to continuing these conversations, sharing experiences, and collaborating with the SAP community at future events.

 

#SAPCommunity#SAPGeorgia#Tbilisi#Georgiatech #SAPS4HANA #DigitalTransformation #CloudTransformation #EnterpriseTransformation #Innovation #SAPConsulting #Leadership #Technology #Networking
